objectives: Identify tasks that can run concurrently because they have no data dependency.
Explain the benefits and trade-offs of concurrency, including race conditions and deadlock.
Apply concurrent thinking to an unseen scenario, weighing one benefit against one trade-off.

retrieval.q: When can two tasks safely run concurrently?
retrieval.a: When they are independent — they share no data, so neither depends on the other's result.
When the result depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent tasks that share data.
When tasks each wait for a resource the other holds, so none can proceed.

retrieval.q: On a single core, what does 'concurrent' really mean?
retrieval.a: Time-slicing — the processor rapidly switches between tasks rather than running them truly simultaneously.

retrieval.q: Interleaving (2.1.3): how does a data dependency limit concurrency?
retrieval.a: A step needing an earlier step's output must run after it, so dependent steps cannot be parallelised.

keywords.def: Spotting parts of a problem that can run at the same time (in parallel or interleaved).
Running multiple tasks in overlapping time, by true parallelism or time-slicing.
Multiple tasks running literally simultaneously on multiple cores.
A single core rapidly switching between tasks so they appear to run together.
When one task needs another's output, forcing them to run sequentially.
When the outcome depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent tasks sharing data.
When tasks each hold a resource the other needs and neither releases it, so both wait forever.
Coordinating concurrent tasks and recombining their results safely.

content.heading: What concurrency is
points: Running parts of a problem at the same time, in parallel or interleaved.
True parallelism needs multiple cores; a single core time-slices.
Only independent tasks (no shared data) are safe to run together.

content.heading: Benefits
points: Faster completion and better throughput on multi-core hardware.
More responsive programs — the UI stays free while a task runs.
Example: a file download runs while the rest of the program keeps responding.

content.heading: Trade-offs
points: Harder to design, program and debug.
Risk of race conditions and deadlock when tasks share resources.
Results often need synchronising and recombining.

content.heading: Not always faster
points: On a single core there is no true parallelism, only time-slicing.
Creating and managing threads adds overhead.
Some problems are inherently sequential due to data dependencies.

content.heading: Exam technique
points: Check for data dependencies before claiming tasks can run together.
When asked to discuss, pair a benefit with a trade-off.
Apply to the scenario's actual tasks, not generic statements.

worked_example.title: Handling a photo upload with concurrent thinking
steps: List the tasks: (a) generate three thumbnail sizes, (b) scan the image for prohibited content, (c) save the original to storage.
Check dependencies: all three work from the same uploaded image and do not need each other's output, so they are largely independent.
Decide concurrency: (a), (b) and (c) can run at the same time.
Note a sequencing point: the photo should not be published until the content scan result is checked, even if the work runs in parallel.
Benefit: running them together makes the upload feel faster and keeps the app responsive across cores.
Trade-off: concurrency is harder to debug and risks race conditions if tasks touch shared storage; on limited hardware it may not be truly faster.

task: An online store builds a sales report by (i) totalling each region's sales and (ii) combining the regional totals into a grand total. Explain which part can run concurrently and which must run afterwards, referring to data dependency, then give one benefit of doing the regional work concurrently.
challenge: A shared online ticket-booking system risks selling the same seat twice under concurrency. Explain how this race condition arises, and describe one way to prevent it, noting the trade-off your fix introduces.

exit_ticket.q: Which is safe to run concurrently and why: (A) rendering then exporting that same clip, or (B) generating audio for scene 1 and scene 2?
exit_ticket.a: B — scene 1 and scene 2 audio share no data so they are independent; A has a dependency (export needs the rendered clip).

exit_ticket.q: Two threads update a shared bank balance without coordination and the final figure depends on timing. Name the problem.
Give one reason concurrency is not always faster.
exit_ticket.a: On a single core it is only time-slicing, not true parallelism; thread overhead and inherently sequential steps also limit any gain.
